summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is sin(pi/6)?

On the unit circle, pi/6 (30 degrees) corresponds to the point (sqrt(3)/2, 1/2). Since sine is the y-coordinate, sin(pi/6) = 1/2.

What is cos(pi/3)?

On the unit circle, pi/3 (60 degrees) corresponds to the point (1/2, sqrt(3)/2). Since cosine is the x-coordinate, cos(pi/3) = 1/2.

What is tan(pi/4)?

tan(pi/4) = sin(pi/4) / cos(pi/4) = (sqrt(2)/2) / (sqrt(2)/2) = 1. At 45 degrees, sine and cosine are equal, making tangent equal to 1.

What is sin(pi/2)?

At pi/2 (90 degrees), the point on the unit circle is (0, 1). Since sine is the y-coordinate, sin(pi/2) = 1.

What is cos(pi)?

At pi (180 degrees), the point on the unit circle is (-1, 0). Since cosine is the x-coordinate, cos(pi) = -1.

What is sin(5pi/6)?

5pi/6 is in Quadrant II with reference angle pi/6. Sine is positive in Quadrant II, so sin(5pi/6) = sin(pi/6) = 1/2.

What is cos(7pi/6)?

7pi/6 is in Quadrant III with reference angle pi/6. Cosine is negative in Quadrant III, so cos(7pi/6) = -cos(pi/6) = -sqrt(3)/2.

What is the value of tan(3pi/4)?

3pi/4 is in Quadrant II with reference angle pi/4. Tangent is negative in Quadrant II, so tan(3pi/4) = -tan(pi/4) = -1.

What is the unit circle?

The unit circle is a circle centered at the origin (0, 0) with radius 1. It is the fundamental tool for defining trigonometric functions for all angles, not just acute angles in triangles.

What is the secant function?

The secant function is the reciprocal of cosine: sec(theta) = 1/cos(theta). It is undefined wherever cosine equals zero (at pi/2 + n*pi for integer n).

What is the cosecant function?

The cosecant function is the reciprocal of sine: csc(theta) = 1/sin(theta). It is undefined wherever sine equals zero (at n*pi for integer n).

What is the cotangent function?

The cotangent function is the reciprocal of tangent: cot(theta) = cos(theta)/sin(theta) = 1/tan(theta). It is undefined wherever sine equals zero.

What is a 'special angle' in trigonometry?

Special angles (30, 45, 60 degrees or pi/6, pi/4, pi/3 radians) have exact trigonometric values involving simple fractions and square roots. These values come from the 30-60-90 and 45-45-90 right triangles.

A surveyor needs to find the height of a building. Standing 50 meters from the base, the angle of elevation to the top is 60 degrees. Using tan(60) = sqrt(3), what is the height of the building?

Using tan(60) = opposite/adjacent = height/50: height = 50 * tan(60) = 50 * sqrt(3) = 50sqrt(3) meters, approximately 86.6 meters.

A helicopter hovers at a point such that the angle of depression to a landing pad 200 meters away horizontally is 45 degrees. What is the helicopter's altitude? Use tan(45) = 1.

The angle of depression equals the angle of elevation from the pad. Using tan(45) = altitude/200: altitude = 200 * tan(45) = 200 * 1 = 200 meters.

A pendulum swings to a maximum angle of pi/6 radians from the vertical. If the pendulum is 2 meters long, what is the horizontal displacement at maximum swing? Use sin(pi/6) = 1/2.

The horizontal displacement = length * sin(angle) = 2 * sin(pi/6) = 2 * (1/2) = 1 meter. The pendulum moves 1 meter horizontally from the rest position.

A Ferris wheel has a radius of 20 meters and its center is 25 meters above the ground. When a rider is at an angle of 2pi/3 from the rightmost point (3 o'clock), what is their height above the ground? Use sin(2pi/3) = sqrt(3)/2.

Height = center height + radius * sin(angle) = 25 + 20 * sin(2pi/3) = 25 + 20 * (sqrt(3)/2) = 25 + 10sqrt(3) meters, approximately 42.3 meters.

An airplane takes off at a 15-degree angle. After flying 2000 meters along its flight path, what is its altitude? Use sin(15) approximately equal to 0.259.

Altitude = flight distance * sin(angle) = 2000 * sin(15) = 2000 * 0.259 = 518 meters. The airplane reaches an altitude of approximately 518 meters.
